pattern: (?:ModuleNotFoundError: No module named '[VAR]')|(?:ValueError: Please install the '[VAR]' package to use this function)|ERROR: pip's dependency resolver does not currently
description: The Python script failed because a required module or package is not installed in the execution environment, or there are conflicting dependencies.
root_cause: A Python dependency is missing from the Airflow task's environment, or there are dependency version conflicts preventing proper import. This can occur when requirements.txt is not updated, or when the base environment image lacks required packages.
resolution_steps: Identify the missing module name from the error message (e.g., 'xgboost', 'statsmodels', 'db-dtypes')
Check if there's a pip dependency conflict error indicating version incompatibilities
For self-scheduling jobs: Update requirements.txt with exact version (e.g., package==1.2.3)
If dependency conflict: Update requirements.txt to resolve conflicts (pip check, pipdeptree)
Test locally: Create fresh kernel environment, install all dependencies, run script to see everythin is working fine
Edit the Job Configuration from Scheduling Webserver and submit.
Request job re-execution through 1:1 inquiry
prevention_tips: Pin all dependency versions in requirements.txt (package==1.2.3 format)
Use pip-tools (pip-compile) to resolve dependency conflicts before deployment
Test in environment matching production (same Python version, base image)
Implement dependency validation in CI/CD pipeline (pip check, pip-audit)
Document all required dependencies in job configuration.
Use Docker images with pinned base image versions for consistency
representative_examples: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'xgboost'
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'statsmodels'
ValueError: Please install the 'db-dtypes' package to use this function
ERROR: pip's dependency resolver does not currently take into account all the packages that are installed

pattern: The triggering tables has not been updated for more than time limit
description: The job failed because a dependent 'triggering table' was not updated within the specified time limit.
root_cause: The job is configured to wait for the arrival of new data partitions or updates in the reference table before execution. However, no new partitions or data changes were detected within the specified timeout period. This failure typically indicates not only a potential delay or failure in the upstream job but also a possible absence of source data ingestion into the triggering table.
resolution_steps: Check if data of reference table was updated if you set reference tables when you update job
Verify the upstream job responsible for updating the triggering table executed successfully
Check the fin history (update history) of the reference table
If you want to work regardless of the triggering table update, remove the table from 'trigger_tables' in Metadata.yaml
Verify the triggering table configuration is correct
Request job re-execution through 1:1 inquiry after verifying upstream job status
prevention_tips: Monitor upstream job execution times to ensure they complete within timeout windows
Set appropriate timeout values in job configuration based on historical data
Review and adjust trigger_tables configuration regularly
Use dependency management to ensure jobs execute in correct order
representative_examples: The triggering tables has not been updated for more than time limit.

pattern: NameError: name '[^']+' is not defined
description: The Python script failed because it tried to use a variable (often a DataFrame) before it was assigned a value.
root_cause: A logical error in the script where a variable was used before it was initialized. This can happen if an earlier step that was supposed to create the variable failed, was skipped due to conditional logic, or if there is a typo in the variable name.
resolution_steps: Examine the Python script at the specified line number
Ensure the variable is always assigned a value before it's used
Check conditional statements or try-except blocks that might skip variable initialization
Verify that all upstream data processing steps correctly produce the data intended for the variable
An empty or unexpected input might prevent it from being initialized
Add debugging: Temporarily add print statements or logging to trace the value of the variable or related variables
Check for typos in the variable name
Re-deploy the updated job and request a re-execution
prevention_tips: Initialize variables with default values before conditional logic
Use assertions to verify variables are assigned before use
Implement comprehensive input validation
Add logging at key points to track variable states
Use static analysis tools (pylint, mypy) to catch potential issues
Write unit tests that verify variable initialization in all code paths
representative_examples:
Provided dataframe is not defined inside the code (Can also mean that code exited before reaching the dataframe declaration)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/self-scheduling-job/job.py", line 554, in <module>
    final_df.head()
    ^^^^^^^^
NameError: name 'final_df' is not defined

Provided dataframe is not defined inside the code (Can also mean that code exited before reaching the dataframe declaration)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/self-scheduling-job/job.py", line 602, in <module>
    anomaly_df.site_cd.unique()
    ^^^^^^^^^^
NameError: name 'anomaly_df' is not defined

pattern: KeyError: '[^']+'\n(?:.|\n)*?pandas/core/indexes/base\.py[\s\S]*?KeyError: '[^']+']
description: The script failed with a KeyError while trying to access a non-existent column in a pandas DataFrame.
root_cause: The script expects a column with a specific name that is not present in the DataFrame. This is usually caused by an upstream data schema change, an error in a data preparation step, or a typo in the column name within the code.
resolution_steps: Examine the error message to identify which column is missing
Review the DataFrame's schema using df.info() and df.columns.tolist()
Check if the column name has a typo (case sensitivity, extra spaces, underscores)
Verify upstream data processing steps that should create or rename the column
Check if there's conditional logic that might prevent the column from being created
Look for errors in data loading or merging operations that could lead to missing columns
Test locally with representative input data to reproduce the error
Correct the column name, ensure it's created, or update the code to use the correct column name
Re-deploy the job and request a re-execution
prevention_tips: Implement schema validation checks before accessing DataFrame columns
Use df.get(column, default_value) instead of df[column] for safer access
Add assertions or validation to ensure required columns exist
Document expected DataFrame schemas in code comments or documentation
Test with various data scenarios to catch edge cases
Use column existence checks before accessing: if 'column_name' in df.columns

pattern: ValueError: month must be in 1\.\.12
description: The script failed with a ValueError when trying to create a date or time object with invalid parameters (e.g., month 0).
root_cause: A logical error in date/time calculation logic. A common case is subtracting from a month or day without correctly handling rollovers (e.g., `month - 1` for January results in 0, which is invalid).
resolution_steps: Review the date calculation logic in the script
Examine the line where the date object is being created
Identify the arithmetic operation causing the invalid value (e.g., today.month-1)
Use a robust method to calculate the new date
Recommended: Use dateutil.relativedelta for safe date arithmetic: from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta; new_date = today - relativedelta(months=1)
Alternative: Implement custom logic that wraps around years (if month-1 is 0, set month to 12 and decrement year)
Test edge cases specifically, like January (where month-1 would be 0)
Correct the date calculation, re-deploy the job, and request a re-execution
prevention_tips: Use dateutil.relativedelta for safe date arithmetic instead of manual calculation
Add validation to ensure date values are within valid ranges before creating date objects
Test date logic with edge cases (month rollovers, leap years, month ends)
Consider using pandas date functions which handle edge cases automatically
Add logging to trace date calculations during development
Write unit tests for date calculation logic with various edge cases
representative_examples:
Provided dataframe is not defined inside the code (Can also mean that code exited before reaching the dataframe declaration)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/self-scheduling-job/job.py", line 201, in <module>
    start_date = date(today.year, today.month-1, 1)
                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ValueError: month must be in 1..12
BadRequest: 400 Invalid timestamp: Cannot parse timestamp '2023-13-01'

pattern: BadRequest: 400 Syntax error|BadRequest: 400 Query error|BadRequest: 400 Invalid timestamp|BadRequest: 400 Failed to parse input string|BadRequest: 400 Invalid NUMERIC value|BadRequest: 400 Column|BadRequest: 400 Duplicate column names|BadRequest: 400 Unrecognized name|BadRequest: 400 Number of arguments does not match|BadRequest: 400 Could not cast literal|BadRequest: 400 Invalid dataset ID|BadRequest: 400 GET|BadRequest: 400 Name|BadRequest: 400 Queries|BadRequest: 400 Function not found|BadRequest: 400 Correlated subqueries|'reason': 'invalidQuery'
description: The BigQuery SQL query contains syntax errors, data type mismatches, invalid references, or other query construction issues preventing execution.
root_cause: SQL query is malformed due to typos, missing elements, incorrect keywords, mismatched parentheses, invalid data types, references to non-existent objects, or incorrect function usage. Often caused by rapid development without proper testing or schema changes.
resolution_steps: Locate the specific SQL query causing error in DAG or task logs
Copy the exact query and error message
Test in Superset console with sample data to isolate the issue
Review for common issues:
- Typos in table names, column names, or function names
- Missing commas in SELECT lists or WHERE clauses
- Mismatched parentheses in complex expressions
- Incorrect data types in comparisons or functions
- References to dropped tables or columns (check schema)
- Invalid date/timestamp formats
- Unquoted or incorrectly quoted identifiers
Fix the identified issues in the query
Test corrected query in Superset console with full data
Update the SQL query within Airflow DAG or configuration
Redeploy the job
Request job re-execution through 1:1 inquiry
prevention_tips: Use SQL linters and formatters (SQLFluff, BigQuery IDE plugin)
Test all queries in Superset console before deployment
Implement SQL validation in CI/CD pipeline (Dry Run mode)
Document expected table schemas and data types
Use parameterized queries to avoid injection issues
Implement query templates with standard patterns
Review query execution plans for optimization opportunities
Use WITH clauses for complex queries to improve readability
representative_examples: google.api_core.exceptions.BadRequest: 400 Syntax error: Expected end of input but got identifier \"xxxxxx\" at [1:9]
BadRequest: 400 Invalid timestamp: Cannot parse timestamp '2023-13-01'
BadRequest: 400 Unrecognized name: non_existent_column
BadRequest: 400 Column not found: project.dataset.table
BadRequest: 400 Function not found: INVALID_FUNCTION_NAME

pattern: KeyError: 'hour_gmt_[0-9]{1,2}'\n(?:.|\n)*?KeyError: 'hour_gmt_[0-9]{1,2}'
description: The script failed with a KeyError while accessing a dynamically named column (e.g., 'hour_gmt_14') that was expected to exist.
root_cause: The script dynamically generates column names based on time or other parameters, but the expected data for that specific partition (e.g., a certain hour) was not generated or loaded, leading to the absence of the corresponding column.
resolution_steps: Identify which dynamically named column is missing from the error
Check the expected hour or time partition value that should have generated the column
Verify upstream data sources to confirm that data for the relevant hour exists
Review the time logic for determining the current hour or constructing the column name
Check for data loading or filtering steps that might exclude necessary hourly data
Add column existence check before access: if f'hour_gmt_{hour}' in df.columns
Implement default handling for missing hourly columns if appropriate
Test locally with debug logging to trace the DataFrame's state before the KeyError
Update job to handle missing hourly data or fix data generation logic
Re-deploy and request re-execution
prevention_tips: Add validation to ensure expected hourly columns exist before access
Implement safe column access using df.get() with default values
Add logging to track which hourly columns are being generated
Test with various execution times to cover all hour scenarios
Consider using pivot tables or melts instead of dynamic columns
Document expected hourly data patterns in job documentation

pattern: InternalServerError: 500 Operation timed out|The data processing capacity of 'Create statement' query has exceeded the limit
description: The query failed because the volume of data being processed or written by a 'Create statement' exceeded the system's limit.
root_cause: The query is inefficiently written, processing an excessive amount of data at once. This often happens with queries that do full table scans or overwrites instead of incremental updates, or lack sufficient filtering (e.g., 'WHERE' clauses).
resolution_steps: Review the query to identify which table is causing the throughput issue
Check if the query is doing a full table scan or processing excessive data
Apply optimization strategies:
- Rather than extracting and overwriting data for entire period, append data for required period to existing table
- Extract only the required period and columns
- Divide the table by purpose/register multiple scheduling jobs instead of containing all columns
- Write query logic efficiently: Use 'JOIN' after applying 'WHERE' and 'GROUP BY'
- Add date range filters in WHERE clauses to limit data processed
- Use partition pruning by filtering on partition columns
Test optimized query in Superset console to verify it stays within limits
Update job with optimized query
Redeploy and request re-execution
prevention_tips: Always add WHERE clauses with date ranges to limit data volume
Use incremental loading strategies instead of full table overwrites
Partition tables by date for better query performance
Monitor query execution time and data volume in Superset console
Use EXPLAIN to analyze query execution plans before deployment
Test queries with sample data to identify throughput issues early
representative_examples: InternalServerError: 500 Operation timed out
The data processing capacity of 'Create statement' query has exceeded the limit.

pattern: BadRequest: 400 Resources exceeded|InternalServerError: 500 Request timed out|AirflowException: Pod|kubernetes.client.exceptions.ApiException|There's a temporary lack of resources
description: The job failed due to a temporary lack of available computing resources in the execution environment.
root_cause: The execution cluster or environment experienced a temporary spike in demand or a transient issue, leaving insufficient CPU, memory, or other resources to schedule or run the task pod.
resolution_steps: Wait a few minutes and retry the job execution (this is typically a transient issue)
Check the Kubernetes cluster status for resource availability issues
Verify there are no widespread outages affecting the execution environment
If the issue persists across multiple retries, check if your job is requesting excessive resources
Review executor_config or kubernetes_executor_conf settings in your DAG
Consider adjusting resource requests/limits if they are set too high
Monitor for node capacity issues or scheduling problems
Submit 1:1 inquiry to request job re-execution if issue doesn't resolve
prevention_tips: Set appropriate resource requests and limits based on actual job requirements
Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures
Schedule resource-intensive jobs during off-peak hours
Monitor cluster resource utilization and usage patterns
Use resource quotas to prevent any single job from consuming all resources
representative_examples: BadRequest: 400 Resources exceeded
InternalServerError: 500 Request timed out
AirflowException: Pod job-12345 returned a failure
kubernetes.client.exceptions.ApiException
There's a temporary lack of resources.

pattern: Job execution was cancelled: Job timed out after|The query execution time has exceeded the time limit
description: The SQL query execution time exceeded the configured time limit.
root_cause: The query is too complex, processes a very large volume of data without proper optimization, or is stuck due to resource contention, causing it to run longer than the maximum allowed execution time.
resolution_steps: Review the query complexity and data volume being processed
Apply optimization strategies to reduce execution time:
- Append data for required period to existing table rather than full overwrite
- Extract only the required period and columns
- Divide the table by purpose/register multiple scheduling jobs
- Write query logic efficiently: Use 'JOIN' after applying 'WHERE' and 'GROUP BY'
- Add date range filters to limit data processed
- Use partitioning and clustering for better performance
Test optimized query in Superset console to verify it completes within time limits
Consider increasing timeout if optimization is not possible and query is necessary
Update job with optimized query
Redeploy and request re-execution
prevention_tips: Always test queries with realistic data volumes before deployment
Use EXPLAIN to analyze query performance and identify bottlenecks
Partition tables by date for efficient date-range queries
Monitor query execution times in production and optimize slow queries
Set appropriate timeout values based on historical performance data
Use materialized views or pre-aggregated tables for complex queries
representative_examples: Job execution was cancelled: Job timed out after 1800 seconds
The query execution time has exceeded the time limit

pattern: Forbidden: 403 Access Denied|PermissionDenied: 403
description: The job failed due to insufficient permissions to access a required resource.
root_cause: The service account or user running the Airflow task lacks the necessary IAM roles or permissions to read, write, or execute an operation on a required resource (e.g., a GCS bucket, BigQuery table, or other cloud service).
resolution_steps: Identify which resource (BigQuery table, S3 bucket, etc.) cannot be accessed
Check the specific permission or IAM role that is missing from the error message
Verify the service account being used by the Airflow task
Request the platform team to grant the necessary IAM permissions
If using self-scheduling jobs, ensure your service account has appropriate roles
Common roles needed: BigQuery Data Viewer, BigQuery Data Editor, Storage Object Viewer, etc.
After permissions are granted, request job re-execution through 1:1 inquiry
Test access permissions manually if possible before re-executing
prevention_tips: Document required IAM roles for each job in job documentation
Use least privilege principle: grant only necessary permissions
Test service account permissions in development environment before production
Implement IAM permission validation in CI/CD pipeline
Use service account impersonation for better security
Regularly audit IAM roles and permissions to ensure they are appropriate
representative_examples: Forbidden: 403 Access Denied
google.api_core.exceptions.PermissionDenied: 403 User does not have permission to query table [VAR], or table does not exist.

pattern: BadRequest: 400 PATCH|BadRequest: 400 No matching signature|BadRequest: 400 POST|BadRequest: 400 Invalid schema update|BadRequest: 400 Invalid value|BadRequest: 400 Provided Schema does not match|Invalid schema update|The schema of the scheduling result table does not match the schema of the existing table
description: The job failed because the schema of the query result does not match the schema of the destination table.
root_cause: When appending or overwriting data, the number of columns, column names, data types, or column order of the new data does not match the existing target table's schema, leading to a write failure.
resolution_steps: Extract the schema comparison error from the logs to identify mismatch details
Check source table schema: SELECT * FROM project.dataset.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE table_name = 'source_table'
Check destination table schema: SELECT * FROM project.dataset.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE table_name = 'dest_table'
Compare schemas and identify differences: missing columns, extra columns, data type mismatches, column name differences, column order differences
Fix the mismatch by one of these methods:
- Method A: Modify query to match destination schema (add CAST, rename columns, drop extra columns)
- Method B: Alter destination table to accept new schema (if acceptable)
- Method C: Use CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE to overwrite entire table with new schema
Test corrected query in Superset console with sample data
Update job with corrected query or table alteration
Redeploy and re-execute
prevention_tips: Always validate schemas before production deployment using INFORMATION_SCHEMA
Implement schema validation in CI/CD pipeline with automated checks
Use schema evolution strategies: versioned tables, schema registry, or schema-on-read
Document table schemas in job documentation or data dictionary
Use schema versioning in table names (e.g., table_v1, table_v2)
Implement schema diff tools to catch changes before deployment
Consider using Dataform or dbt for schema management
Add explicit CAST statements in queries to ensure type consistency
representative_examples: BadRequest: 400 PATCH - Schema does not match. Destination table has 5 columns, source has 6
BadRequest: 400 Invalid schema update. Cannot add column 'new_column' to table
BadRequest: 400 Provided Schema does not match. Field 'amount' has changed type from INT64 to FLOAT64
pyarrow.lib.ArrowTypeError: Could not convert FLOAT64 column to INT64
The schema of the scheduling result table does not match the schema of the existing table.

pattern: File "[VAR]/site-packages/google/cloud/bigquery/_pandas_helpers.py", line [VAR], in augment_schema[\s\S]*File "pyarrow/error\.pxi", line [VAR], in pyarrow\.lib\.check_status
description: The job failed while automatically inferring a BigQuery schema from a pandas DataFrame.
root_cause: The BigQuery client library, using PyArrow, could not determine the correct BigQuery data types for one or more columns in the DataFrame. This is often due to ambiguous types (e.g., 'object' columns with mixed data), nested structures, or unsupported pandas data types.
resolution_steps: Review DataFrame schema using df.info() and df.head() to identify problematic columns
Look for columns with ambiguous types (e.g., 'object' columns containing mixed data)
Check for nested structures (lists, dicts) in the DataFrame
Handle mixed types: Ensure each column has a consistent data type
Convert DataFrame columns to appropriate types before upload using df.astype() or pd.to_datetime()
Handle nested data: Flatten lists/dicts to separate columns or use STRUCT/ARRAY types in BigQuery schema
Instead of relying on automatic schema inference, provide explicit schema using SchemaField objects
Test data conversion with sample data reflecting full range of expected types
Update job with pre-processed data and explicit schema
Re-deploy and re-execute
prevention_tips: Always perform data type validation on DataFrames before BigQuery uploads
For critical tables, define fixed BigQuery schemas and use them instead of inference
Consistently convert data types before upload (e.g., all numeric to float64, dates to datetime64)
Test with sample data covering all edge cases of type variations
Use df.convert_dtypes() for consistent type conversion
Document expected data types in job documentation

pattern: AirflowSensorTimeout|DELIVERY_TIMEOUT: Execution time has exceeded the time limit
description: The task's data delivery operation exceeded its execution time limit.
root_cause: The process of writing or delivering the final dataset to its destination (e.g., an external storage system) took longer than the configured timeout. This can be caused by network latency, a very large result set, or a slow destination system.
resolution_steps: Check the destination type of your job (external storage, BigQuery, etc.)
Verify that query result is being updated to external storage if destination is external storage
Check the size of the result set being delivered (may be too large)
Review network connectivity to the destination system
Check if the destination system is experiencing slowness or issues
Consider optimizing the query to reduce result set size
Increase timeout value if appropriate for the job's requirements
For external storage, check if the storage service has rate limits or throttling
Request job re-execution through 1:1 inquiry after addressing the issue
prevention_tips: Monitor data delivery times in production and set appropriate timeouts
Optimize queries to reduce result set size before delivery
Use incremental delivery strategies for large datasets
Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for delivery operations
Monitor destination system performance and capacity
Use streaming delivery for very large datasets instead of batch
representative_examples: AirflowSensorTimeout
Execution time has exceeded the time limit.

pattern: airflow\.exceptions\.AirflowException: Pod [VAR] returned a failure:
description: The Airflow task failed because its underlying Kubernetes pod terminated with an error before completion.
root_cause: An infrastructure-level problem with the Kubernetes pod responsible for task execution. Causes include insufficient memory (OOMKilled), node failures, persistent volume claim issues, container image pull errors, or pod configuration errors.
resolution_steps: Check the full Kubernetes Pod logs (not just Airflow task logs) for specific error messages
Look for container exit codes, OOMKilled events, or initialization failures
Review executor_config or kubernetes_executor_conf settings in your DAG
Check resource requests and limits: ensure pod's requested CPU and memory are sufficient
Verify volume mounts (especially EXTRA_FILES) are correctly configured and accessible
Confirm the container image can be pulled successfully from the registry
Investigate Kubernetes cluster health: node capacity, networking issues, cloud provider status
If the problem is intermittent, try re-running the task
If issue persists, escalate to platform team or Kubernetes administrators
Request job re-execution through 1:1 inquiry after infrastructure issues are resolved
prevention_tips: Set appropriate resource requests and limits based on historical usage
Implement robust resource monitoring and alerting
Regularly monitor Kubernetes cluster health and resource utilization
Ensure external storage and dependencies are highly available and properly configured
Test job configuration in development environment before production deployment
Use health checks and readiness probes in container configuration

pattern: google\.api_core\.retry\.retry_unary\.py[\s\S]*_retry_error_helper
description: The job failed after multiple unsuccessful attempts to call the BigQuery API.
root_cause: The BigQuery client library's internal retry mechanism failed to complete an API call after several attempts. This typically points to transient issues like network instability, temporary BigQuery service unavailability, or hitting API rate limits.
resolution_steps: Check BigQuery service status via Google Cloud Status Dashboard
Verify network connectivity from Airflow environment to Google Cloud endpoints
Review BigQuery quotas to check if hitting API rate limits (tables.get calls, etc.)
If hitting quotas, consider requesting a quota increase or optimizing operations to reduce API calls
For transient issues, wait a short period and re-run the job
Check if there are concurrent jobs overwhelming the API
Review the job's BigQuery usage patterns for optimization opportunities
Implement error handling to distinguish between quota errors and other failures
Request job re-execution through 1:1 inquiry after verifying service health
prevention_tips: Implement robust logging for BigQuery API interactions including response codes
Design jobs to be idempotent for safe re-execution
Optimize BigQuery operations to minimize API calls and avoid hitting quotas
Use BigQuery query caching to reduce redundant queries
Monitor API usage to detect quota exhaustion early
Implement circuit breaker pattern to handle service outages gracefully

pattern: ValidationError|exception.BaseException|Bad response from application: 500|Bad response from delivery service|Failed to transfer some files
description: The job failed due to an unexpected error during data delivery to an external system.
root_cause: An unknown or unhandled exception occurred during the data delivery operation. This is a catch-all for delivery errors that don't fit into more specific categories and often require deeper investigation of the full logs.
resolution_steps: Review the full error logs and traceback for specific error details
Identify the external system where delivery failed (check destination type in job configuration)
Check if the external system is experiencing service issues or outages
Verify network connectivity to the external system
Check if the data format being delivered is compatible with the external system requirements
Review authentication credentials or API keys for the external system
Check if the external system has sufficient storage capacity
If the issue is unclear, collect all relevant error information and logs
Submit 1:1 inquiry with detailed error information for investigation
Request job re-execution after the issue is identified and resolved
prevention_tips: Test delivery to external systems in development environment before production
Implement comprehensive error handling and logging for delivery operations
Monitor external system status and availability
Use data format validation before attempting delivery
Implement retry logic for transient delivery failures
Document external system integration points and requirements
representative_examples: ValidationError
exception.BaseException
Bad response from application: 500
Bad response from delivery service
Failed to transfer some files

pattern: NotFound: 404 Not found: Table|NotFound: 404 Not found: Dataset|NotFound: 404 GET|FileNotFoundError: \[Errno 2\] No such file or directory: '[VAR]'|Table or view not found: `[VAR]`|Table not found: `[VAR]`|relation "[VAR]" does not exist
description: The job failed because it tried to access a file, table, or other resource that does not exist at the specified path.
root_cause: The referenced resource (file, directory, table, dataset) is either misspelled, located in a different database/schema or path, or has been deleted. This is a common logic error in data pipelines and file operations.
resolution_steps: Identify which resource is not found from the error message (file path, table name, dataset name)
For files: Double-check the file path against the expected location in the execution environment
For tables/datasets: Verify the resource exists in the correct project and dataset
Check for typos in the resource name or path
For files: Confirm the file was correctly staged or mounted in the execution environment
For files: Check file permissions and ensure the Airflow task can read the file
Be aware of the current working directory of the Airflow task (use absolute paths if needed)
For tables: Use Superset console to verify the table/dataset exists and is accessible
Check if the resource was recently deleted or renamed
Correct the path or name, or ensure the resource exists, then redeploy and re-execute
prevention_tips: Use absolute paths instead of relative paths for file operations
Implement existence checks before accessing files: if os.path.exists(path)
Test file paths in development environment that matches production structure
Document required files, tables, and datasets in job documentation
Use environment variables for configurable paths and resource names
Implement validation to check resource existence before job execution
For BigQuery, use INFORMATION_SCHEMA to verify tables exist before querying
representative_examples: FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'df_val_samples_test2_scores_raw.csv'
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/home/jovyan/facebook_m2m100_418M'
Table not found: `my_project.my_dataset.deleted_table`
NotFound: 404 Not found: Dataset my-project:my-dataset
relation "non_existent_table" does not exist

pattern: Provided dataframe is not defined inside the code [\s\S]*?NameError: name '(?:cd|get_ipython)' is not defined
description: The script failed with a NameError because it contained code specific to an interactive Jupyter/IPython environment, such as `get_ipython()` or shell commands like `cd`.
root_cause: Code developed in a Jupyter Notebook was moved to a production Python script without removing or converting notebook-specific 'magic' commands or functions, which are not defined in a standard Python interpreter.
resolution_steps: Identify the Jupyter-specific code from the error message (cd, get_ipython, etc.)
If the command is for installing dependencies (e.g., get_ipython().system('pip install')):
- Remove the installation from the script
- Manage dependencies through requirements.txt instead
- Do not try to install packages at runtime within the job
If it's a shell command (like cd):
- Replace with Python equivalent (e.g., os.chdir('/path/to/directory') instead of cd /path)
- Or use subprocess module: subprocess.run(['ls', '-l']) or subprocess.run('ls -l', shell=True)
- Be cautious with shell=True due to security implications
If the code is purely for interactive debugging or environment setup, remove it entirely
Ensure development and testing is done in environment matching production execution
Re-deploy the job and request a re-execution
prevention_tips: Never move Jupyter notebook code directly to production without review
Remove all magic commands (%run, %timeit, etc.) before deployment
Replace shell commands with Python equivalents (os, subprocess, pathlib)
Remove get_ipython() calls and IPython-specific code
Test scripts in standard Python environment before production deployment
Use linters or static analysis tools to identify Jupyter-specific patterns

    get_ipython().system('pip install openpyxl')
    ^^^^^^^^^^^
NameError: name 'get_ipython' is not defined

pattern: KeyError: '[A-Z_0-9]+'\n[\s\S]*?os\.environ\["[A-Z_0-9]+"\]
description: The script failed with a KeyError when trying to access an environment variable that was not set.
root_cause: The code relies on an environment variable (e.g., `os.environ['MY_VAR']`) that is expected to be present in the execution context but was not defined in the Airflow task's environment configuration.
resolution_steps: Identify the missing environment variable name from the error message (e.g., JUPYTERHUB_USER)
Check if the variable is specific to Airflow environment or another platform (e.g., JupyterHub)
If it's Airflow-specific: Ensure it's properly configured in the DAG definition
- Use the env parameter in BashOperator or PythonOperator
- Or configure via Airflow Variables
If the variable is only relevant in JupyterHub and not needed in Airflow, remove the code that tries to access it
For robustness, consider using os.getenv('VARIABLE_NAME', 'default_value') to provide a fallback
- Or use os.getenv('VARIABLE_NAME') followed by an explicit check for None
Update the Airflow DAG/configuration or the script
Re-deploy the job and request a re-execution
prevention_tips: Document all required environment variables in job documentation
Use os.getenv() with defaults for optional environment variables
Add validation at job startup to check required environment variables
Use Airflow Variables for configuration that needs to persist across runs
Test in environment matching production (with same environment variables)
Consider using configuration files instead of relying on environment variables
